reasoning: The recall initiated by LPK1 on February 24, 2025, involved approximately 303 pounds of chicken Caesar wraps distributed solely to vending machines in Washington state. The best-by dates for these products were February 21, 23, and 25 — meaning the products were already past or at their expiration date at the precise time the recall was announced. In cases involving small production runs, concentrated distribution networks (vending machines), and products that have already reached the end of their shelf life, the USDA's Food Safety and Inspection Service (FSIS) can complete recall effectiveness checks very quickly because there is extremely limited remaining product in commerce to recover. Given that the resolution date of June 1, 2025 is more than three months (roughly 97 days) after the recall onset, there is ample time for the FSIS to conclude its effectiveness checks and administratively change the status of the recall from Active to Closed. The only significant risk of it remaining Active is bureaucratic delay on the part of the USDA for a minor, low-priority recall. Thus, the probability of it resolving 'Yes' is high.
